skill.name: binary-re-dynamic-analysis
skill.description: Guides dynamic analysis of binaries with QEMU, GDB, Frida, strace, Docker, and on-device tracing. It emphasizes human approval, sandbox setup, runtime observation, and structured evidence capture.
skill.summary: Run controlled binary dynamic analysis and record runtime evidence.
skill.author: 2389-research

security_audit.summary: Static findings are mostly true positives because the skill intentionally documents commands that execute binaries, start privileged Docker containers, attach debuggers, trace processes, and read runtime files. Several IP address and log path alerts are false positives because they appear in example output, not active code. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found, but publication should require strong warnings and reviewer approval.

high_findings.title: Privileged Docker Execution Guidance
high_findings.description: The skill instructs users to run a Docker container with privileged mode to register emulation handlers. This is legitimate for binfmt setup, but it grants elevated host-level capabilities and can be dangerous with untrusted images or hosts.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The privileged Docker flag is explicit and the surrounding context confirms it is intended to be executed. The command is a known setup pattern, so this is high risk but not malicious by itself.

high_findings.title: Execution of Potentially Untrusted Binaries
high_findings.description: The skill provides many commands for running target binaries under QEMU, GDB, Frida, Docker, and on-device tools. Dynamic binary execution is inherently risky because samples may contain malware, exploit debuggers, or access local files and networks.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands directly execute binaries and attach tracing tools. The skill includes approval and sandbox guidance, which reduces concern about intent but not operational risk.

medium_findings.title: Remote Debugging and File Transfer Workflows
medium_findings.description: The skill documents remote debugging over SSH and copying trace logs from a target device. These workflows can expose target systems or transfer sensitive runtime logs if used without authorization and isolation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The remote commands are clear and intentional, but they are standard reverse-engineering workflows. Risk depends on authorization and target environment controls.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem and Process Introspection Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to inspect /proc indicators, dump memory, source a local GDB file, and collect file access evidence. These are expected analysis activities, but they can expose sensitive host or target data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file and process references are explicit. The context is legitimate malware or firmware analysis, so this is a controlled-use concern rather than evidence of abuse.

medium_findings.title: Container Mutation and Package Installation Examples
medium_findings.description: The Docker example creates a linker symlink and installs packages inside the container before executing the sample. This is common for dependency recovery, but it increases supply-chain and reproducibility risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are concrete and modify the container runtime state. The risk is moderate because the changes are inside a temporary container in the shown workflow.

low_findings.title: Documentation-Only Network and Log Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded IP addresses and log paths appear in example output and journal templates. They do not show active exfiltration or log deletion in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are inside output examples and narrative journal entries. No evidence found that the skill itself connects to those addresses or deletes logs.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Static Alert Is a Name Collision
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alert appears to be triggered by the word dynamic in the skill name and description. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or cryptographic implementation logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain metadata and a Docker setup comment, not cryptographic code. This is a likely false positive based on token matching.

dangerous_patterns.title: Privileged Container Setup
dangerous_patterns.description: Uses privileged Docker mode for binfmt registration, which can weaken host isolation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The privileged flag is directly present in an executable shell example.

dangerous_patterns.title: Runtime Execution and Debugging Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: Runs binaries with QEMU, GDB, Docker, Frida, and on-device tools, which can execute untrusted code paths.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The skill purpose and examples both confirm real binary execution workflows.

dangerous_patterns.title: Host and Target Filesystem Access
dangerous_patterns.description: Includes examples for sourcing local debugger configuration, dumping memory to /tmp, reading /proc markers, and pulling trace logs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem operations are explicit. They are expected in dynamic analysis but can reveal sensitive runtime data.

content.value_statement: Dynamic binary analysis is risky and hard to document consistently. This skill guides controlled execution, tracing, debugging, and evidence capture for reverse-engineering work.
actual_capabilities: Plans human-approved dynamic analysis before any binary execution.
Guides QEMU user-mode execution with syscall tracing for ARM and ARM64 targets.
Shows GDB and gdb-multiarch workflows for breakpoints, registers, stack, and memory inspection.
Explains Frida hooks for function interception and runtime memory inspection.
Documents Docker and on-device workflows for cross-architecture or device-specific analysis.
Provides structured formats for syscall summaries, observations, hypotheses, and journal entries.
limitations: It is guidance only and does not provide an automated sandbox or enforcement layer.
Execution safety depends on the user validating isolation, network controls, and target authorization.
Frida support is limited to native architecture targets or on-device frida-server workflows.
Some examples require installed tools such as QEMU, GDB, Docker, nsjail, Frida, r2, jq, SSH, and strace.

use_cases.title: Map Unknown Binary Behavior
use_cases.description: Trace syscalls, file access, process activity, and network attempts while preserving an evidence trail.

use_cases.title: Debug Cross-Architecture Firmware Samples
use_cases.description: Run ARM or ARM64 binaries from a Linux or macOS workstation using QEMU, Docker, and gdb-multiarch.

use_cases.title: Validate Static Analysis Hypotheses
use_cases.description: Confirm suspected functions, anti-debug behavior, and runtime connections with controlled tracing and hooks.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Safe Run Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a dynamic analysis plan for this binary. Include required approvals, sandbox settings, network isolation, commands to run, and evidence to capture.

prompt_templates.title: Trace Runtime Behavior
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the dynamic analysis workflow to design a QEMU or strace run. Focus on files, processes, network calls, exit code, and suspicious runtime behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Debug a Specific Function
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a GDB session for this binary and suspected function. Include breakpoints, register inspection, stack inspection, memory dumps, and stopping conditions.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Runtime Evidence to Hypotheses
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these runtime observations and update the analysis hypotheses. Separate confirmed facts, contradicted assumptions, unresolved questions, and follow-up experiments.

output_examples.input: A request to run an ARM firmware daemon under QEMU with network blocked.
output_examples.output: A staged run plan with approval notes, QEMU command selection, timeout limits, expected syscall evidence, and a structured observation template.

output_examples.input: A request to inspect a suspected connect function in a native Linux binary.
output_examples.output: A debugger and Frida plan that names hook targets, breakpoint goals, argument observations, and evidence needed to confirm network behavior.

output_examples.input: Runtime logs from a sandboxed test execution.
output_examples.output: A concise analysis summary that lists observed file access, blocked network attempts, process activity, confidence changes, and next questions.

best_practices: Get explicit human approval before executing any sample or attaching to a target device.
Use network isolation, timeouts, resource limits, and read-only mounts before collecting runtime evidence.
Record commands, sandbox settings, observations, and confidence changes immediately after each experiment.
anti_patterns: Do not run unknown binaries directly on a host workstation without isolation.
Do not use privileged Docker or on-device debugging on systems that contain sensitive data.
Do not treat sample IP addresses, paths, or journal examples as proof without runtime evidence.

faq.question: Does this skill execute binaries by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides commands and workflows. A user or agent with tool access would still need approval and an execution environment.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk level high?
faq.answer: The skill teaches execution, debugging, tracing, privileged Docker setup, and filesystem inspection. These are powerful actions with real operational risk.

faq.question: Is the skill malicious?
faq.answer: No evidence found of malicious intent. The content is legitimate reverse-engineering guidance, but it needs strict safety controls.

faq.question: What tools does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers QEMU, GDB, gdb-multiarch, Frida, Docker, nsjail, strace, r2, jq, SSH, and basic Linux tracing commands.

faq.question: Can it analyze malware samples?
faq.answer: It can guide malware analysis workflows, but only inside approved, isolated, and legally authorized environments.

faq.question: What should users prepare first?
faq.answer: Prepare a sandbox, confirm network policy, define stopping conditions, verify tool availability, and document the approved experiment scope.
